fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.display_name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Large & Mid Cap fund gives a blend of stability from large companies and growth from mid-sized ones. Good for long-term wealth creation.

fund.name: Invesco India Midcap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap funds have higher growth potential than large caps. This fund has a strong 3-year and 5-year track record, suitable for aggressive investors.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Small-cap funds are the highest growth potential but also higher risk. Bandhan has delivered strong 3-year and 5-year returns, fitting your high-risk appetite.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Flexi cap fund offers flexibility to invest across market caps, providing diversification and downside protection while still capturing growth.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Aggressive hybrid fund balances equity with debt, providing some stability while still aiming for growth. Good for moderating overall portfolio volatility.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Gold ETF
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Gold provides diversification and acts as a hedge against inflation and market downturns. Even a small allocation helps in portfolio stability.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU. Step 2: Transfer your existing corpus of ₹25 lakh to these funds as per the lumpsum allocation (₹1.25 lakh in gold ETF). Step 3: Start monthly SIPs of ₹2.85 lakh (₹75k + ₹60k + ₹60k + ₹60k + ₹30k) across these funds. Step 4: For the luxury apartment purchase in 3 years, set aside ₹50 lakh (use existing savings or a separate liquid fund) — this plan focuses on your long-term corpus build-up.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs are running smoothly and adjust if any fund underperforms its category.
After 1 year: Review fund performance vs. category benchmark — replace any fund consistently lagging for 2+ quarters.
After 3 years: Reassess the luxury apartment goal; consider a separate debt fund for that horizon.
Every 2 years: Check asset allocation drift; rebalance if equity share exceeds 90% or falls below 70%.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check the portfolio's equity-debt-gold mix. If equities have grown beyond 85% of the portfolio, book profits and move money to debt or gold. If equities fall below 70%, shift from debt/gold to equity. Use the ELSS fund's lock-in to your advantage — invest the first ₹1.5 lakh via ELSS for tax saving (if not using new regime).
plan.rationale: You're a high-earning, aggressive investor with a 20-year horizon and a ₹25 lakh corpus. This plan focuses on high-growth assets (mid-cap, small-cap, flexi-cap) while adding a hybrid fund for some stability and gold for diversification. The monthly SIP of ₹2.85 lakh will build a strong corpus, and your existing corpus will kickstart growth. The luxury apartment purchase in 3 years requires a separate plan (use liquid/debt funds) — this plan targets your 5Cr goal and financial independence by 45.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal and recovers over time.
Mid-cap and small-cap funds are more volatile than large caps; expect sharper ups and downs.
Gold's high 1-year return (58.9%) is not sustainable — long-term returns average 8-10%.
Inflation and lifestyle costs may outpace your returns — regularly review your goals and SIP amounts.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: The equity allocation is entirely in actively managed funds (large & mid cap, mid cap, small cap, flexi cap) with no index funds or ETFs, so passive_instrument_fraction is 0. The plan suggests annual rebalancing, some performance-based review at 6 months and 1 year, and a 2-year rebalancing frequency, indicating infrequent tactical moves - hence turnover_score is high but not maximum (0.8). Cost is mentioned mainly in expense ratios with values around 1% to 1.2%, but cost minimization is not emphasized as a primary criterion - thus cost_emphasis_score is low (0.3). The rationale talks about fund performance and manager qualities implicitly, indicating deep research into fund selection rather than reliance on broad market indexing, so research_vs_cost_score is low (0.1). The plan targets a 20-year horizon with emphasis on long-term wealth creation, periodic but infrequent reviews and SIP step-ups, supporting a buy-and-hold philosophy - so time_horizon_alignment_score is very high (0.95). Portfolio activeness is strongly active: all funds have alpha (8.55 to 15.21), betas varying from 0.55 to 1.12 but likely active given category and alpha. No passive/closet index funds here, so portfolio_activeness_score is 0.0.

pqs.reasoning: The plan is very well aligned with Jay Gatsby's aggressive risk appetite, long 20-year horizon, and goal of building ₹5Cr in 10 years, plus plans for financial independence by 45. The focus on large & mid-cap, mid-cap, small-cap, flexi-cap, and an aggressive hybrid fund fits his profile and goals effectively. The separate earmarking of funds for the 3-year luxury apartment goal (outside this plan) is appropriate for timeline sensitivity. Diversification is strong across equity segments and gold, but there is no explicit exposure to international or sector diversification or multiple fund houses beyond ICICI Prudential and Invesco/Bandhan/PPFAS, so slightly less than perfect on diversification. The risk-return is appropriate since the investor is aggressive and projections (base 12.8%, bull 16.8%, bear 8.8%) are realistic with clear downside risk warnings. The rationale and allocations, rebalancing rules, risks, and projected returns are fully coherent and consistent. Tax efficiency is good: plan recognizes Jay's 30% slab, suggests ELSS use explicitly for 80C benefits, prefers equity and aggressive hybrid funds which are taxed at favorable equity rates, and acknowledges tax treatment. Slight penalty since ELSS itself is not explicitly named or included in allocations though mentioned in rebalancing guidelines; somewhat relies on aggressive hybrid as partial solution but lacks a direct ELSS fund allocation. Overall, high-quality plan with minor improvement possible on more broad diversification and explicit ELSS inclusion.
